heading: Logical Building Blocks
body: Logic distinguishes between statements that stand alone and those constructed from multiple parts. This distinction depends entirely on the presence of logical operators. Identifying whether a statement is simple or compound is the first step in evaluating its truth value.

heading: Simple Statements
body: A **simple statement** is a proposition that contains no logical operators. It makes a single, atomic claim about the world. In symbolic logic, a simple statement is represented by a single constant or variable, such as $P$ or $Q$. It cannot be broken down into smaller logical units.
note: Simple statements are also known as atomic statements.

heading: Examples of Simple Statements
body: These sentences contain no connectors or modifiers.

examples.text: The sky is blue and the grass is green.

note: The final example contains 'and', which makes it non-simple.

heading: Compound Statements
body: A **compound statement** is a proposition that contains at least one logical operator. It uses operators like 'and', 'or', 'if...then', or 'if and only if' to connect simple statements. A compound statement can also be a single statement modified by a negation.

heading: The Operator Test
body: To identify a compound statement, look for any of the five logical operators: negation ($\sim$), conjunction ($\wedge$), disjunction ($\vee$), conditional ($\rightarrow$), or biconditional ($\leftrightarrow$). If a logical string contains even one of these symbols, it is a compound statement. If it contains none, it is simple.

heading: Negation is an Operator
body: Beginners often mistake negated statements for simple ones because they only involve one subject. However, 'not' is a logical operator. Therefore, any statement containing a negation is compound. $\sim P$ is a compound statement even though it only contains one variable.
note: Both are compound because they contain the negation operator.

heading: Symbolic Identification
body: Apply the rule to symbolic strings to categorize them quickly.
note: $P$, $Q$, and $R$ alone are simple. Any additions make them compound.

heading: Key Takeaway
body: Simple statements contain no operators and represent a single claim. Compound statements contain one or more operators that connect or modify propositions.
